The surgeon begins the procedure by creating a peritoneal flap, starting laterally where there is abundant fat. A lateral peritoneal incision is made, revealing two potential dissection planes. The surgeon opts to stay close to the peritoneum to facilitate later dissection between the peritoneum and testicular vessels. As the dissection progresses medially, the surgeon pushes fat away and approaches the attenuated linea semilunaris to access the preperitoneal and retrorectus planes. Due to the patient's thin peritoneum, the surgeon chooses the retrorectus space for a more robust flap, pulling on the posterior rectus sheath and entering the retrorectus space, characterized by visible fat and the inferior gastric vessel. The surgeon highlights the spaces of Retzius and Bagros, noting the thin peritoneum in Bagros and a thicker posterior flap in Retzius.
